data.title: Knife River: Growth Story With Little Evidence Of Long-Term Value Creation
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Luis Rios as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Knife River Corporation (KNF) appears overvalued relative to its earnings power. Despite strong construction activity, the company’s returns on invested capital remain at or below its cost of capital, suggesting limited long-term value creation.

The recent Q3 2025 earnings report shows costs of revenue continue rising at a higher pace than annual revenue growth, constraining operating margins and reducing the company’s earnings power value. In a fragmented, low-barrier industry, Knife River appears unlikely to develop durable competitive advantages. The cyclicality and seasonality of the business also add another burden to the company, further compressing earnings and ROIC, even if the asset base keeps growing thanks to their M&A strategy.

I rate Knife River Underweight, with a fair price of $55 per diluted share. While near-term revenue benefits from acquisitions and backlog, rising costs, margin pressure, and sub-cost-of-capital returns suggest limited upside at $72/share. The market appears to price a 3–4% long-term revenue CAGR that I view as optimistic for a newly standalone heavy-materials business.

Company Overview

Knife River Corp. is an aggregates-led construction materials and contracting services provider in the United States, based in Bismarck, North Dakota. It operates in 14 states, including Alaska, California, Hawaii, Oregon, Washington, Idaho, Montana, Wyoming, Iowa, Minnesota, North Dakota, South Dakota, Nebraska, and Texas. Knife River possesses 1.2 billion tons of aggregate permitted reserves, 182 active aggregate sites, 106 ready-mix plants, 51 asphalt plants, and 9 liquid asphalt terminals. Its contracting services are mainly provided to public-sector customers (the government) and include asphalt paving, heavy-civil construction (highways, local roads, bridges, and other public infrastructure), concrete construction, and site development. The company also manufactures prestressed concrete products in some segments. Knife River targets mid-size, high-growth markets using a vertically integrated business strategy to obtain a competitive advantage. The company does serve both public and private customers, but most of its revenues are mainly earned from public markets.

The company has six operating segments: Pacific, Northwest, Mountain, North Central, South, and Energy Services. Each segment mines, processes, and sells construction aggregates (crushed stone, sand, and gravel), produces and sells asphalt, and produces and sells ready-mix concrete, as well as vertically integrates its contracting services to support its aggregate-based product lines.

Besides its vertical integration business strategy, Knife River focuses on what they call the EDGE strategy to instill the company’s culture and goals in the newly acquired businesses as part of that integration process. They describe the EDGE strategy as follows:

EDGE strategy—Provides a clear competitive advantage in driving long-term, profitable growth.

•EBITDA margin improvement—Drive sustained adjusted EBITDA margin expansion. 20% EBITDA margin long-term.

•Discipline—Strong balance sheet and disciplined allocation of capital to support long-term profitable and value creation.

•Growth—Organic growth and inorganic growth opportunities-based operations in mid-sized, high-growth markets.

•Excellence—Operational excellence through high-quality training at every level of the company.

The implementation of the EDGE strategy started in 2023.

As part of its M&A strategy, Knife River has acquired more than 80 companies since its foundation in 1917. As of November 5th. In 2025, the company acquired Strata Corporation for a purchase price of $454.0 million, Albina Asphalt from Delaware, a Washington aggregate quarry operation in the West segment, an Oregon aggregates and contracting company, and a Central Minnesota aggregate and contracting services business in the Central segment.

Knife River is a participant in the aggregates, ready-mix concrete, asphalt, liquid asphalt, and contracting services markets, where it competes with Cemex S.A.B. de C.V., CRH plc, Eagle Materials, Inc., Granite Construction, Inc., Heidelberg Materials, Holcim, Martin Marietta Materials, Inc., Construction Partners, Inc., and Vulcan Materials Company.

Situation Overview

Knife River became a standalone company in 2023 after its spin-off from MDU Resources and reported double-digit revenue growth from 2022 to 2023, thanks to the accelerated growth in the construction industry after the COVID-19 pandemic, the low-interest rate environment, and government spending. These factors allowed Knife River to grow revenues and operating margins, and the stock rose from $35 in 2023 to $108 per share in January 2025.

$ in millions

The market had extrapolated the good performance of the previous years and assigned a very high multiple to the EPS for 2023 and 2024. A very optimistic expectation of the future performance was unsustainable, and the market eventually started to correct, and the price dropped from $108 to a low of $64 per share in October 2025. While this 30% drop from the highs in January made the stock look cheaper and perhaps like a buy opportunity, I think the stock is still pricing in too much optimism.

Knife River continues to expand through debt-financed acquisitions. These should help them drive inorganic revenue higher and improve revenue growth. The debt levels are high, and they expect to end 2025 at 2.1x net debt to TTM adjusted EBITDA; however, that is normal for businesses in the construction and aggregate materials industries. But the problem here is not the level of debt but whether these acquisitions will provide profitable growth and create long-term value for investors or will be value destructive by delivering returns below the company’s cost of capital. Current financials, management lowering guidance from $3,450 million in revenues at the beginning of the year to $3,150 million as of the recent earnings report, the nature of its industries (highly competitive, cyclical), and the lack of moat lead me to believe that even if revenues increase, these acquisitions will be value destructive for long-term investors, as operating costs rise, operating margins dwindle, and returns on invested capital continue to disappoint.

Valuation Analysis

I value Knife River using Bruce Greenwald’s intrinsic framework, comparing its Earnings Power Value (EPV) with its Net Asset Value (NAV). This approach distinguishes between the company’s ability to earn sustainable profits and the replacement cost of its assets.

On an equity basis, I estimate Net Asset Value at approximately $2,626.3 million and Earnings Power Value at ~$961.9 million, while on an enterprise basis, I estimate NAV at approximately $3,745 million and EPV at $2,081 million, implying that the market is capitalizing Knife River well above its normalized earning capacity. The large gap between NAV and EPV indicates that the company’s capital is not being utilized efficiently—a typical Case A situation under Greenwald’s classification.

Key Net Asset Value Adjustments

  • Aggregate reserves valued via DCF at $1.49 billion (1.2B tons, 40-year life, 6% price growth, 9% discount rate).
  • Net PP&E adjusted to ~1.62× book ($3.18B), reflecting replacement costs of equipment, buildings, and land.
  • Intangibles and goodwill are retained near the book, as Knife River lacks a durable moat.
  • Current liabilities carried at book value due to short duration.

On the earnings side, I assume sustainable revenue of $2.9 billion, a 9.5% operating margin, and a 26% tax rate, consistent with recent trends (normalized mid-cycle earnings). After adjusting for replacement capex, this results in a normalized NOPAT of ~$187 million, capitalized at a 9% cost of capital to derive an EPV of $2.08 billion.

The average of NAV and EPV, on an equity basis, is $1,749.1 million, divided by 56.9 million diluted shares, yielding a value per diluted share of $31.5, or ~$32 per share, or ~55% below the current market price ($70.99 as of November 7th, 2025).

Even the market enterprise value of $5,350.1 million is far above my intrinsic Net Asset Value estimate of $3,745.4 million and Earning Power Value of $2,081 million. Despite estimating Knife River’s worth under generous assumptions, the market values this company at a substantial premium to the economic worth of its asset base and earnings power.

To cross-check these intrinsic values, I also evaluated Knife River’s returns on invested capital relative to its cost of capital to see whether my findings are consistent in both metrics. Net PP&E in 2025 so far is $1,961.4 million, current assets are $1,135.3 million, cash is $81.1 million, and the total of non-interest-bearing current liabilities is $339.2 million. Then I compute net working capital for 2025, which results in $622 million. Goodwill and intangibles are $469.4 million and $33.4 million. Invested capital for 2025 is $3,086.2 million, and with a NOPAT of $203.9 million, operating ROIC is 6.6%, which is 26% below Knife River’s cost of capital of 9%, further showing this company has no moat, and its assets are being underutilized.

The conclusion is clear: The NAV and EPV discrepancy suggests that Knife River is overinvested relative to its earning capacity, which is a hallmark of companies that try to expand faster than their profitability allows. Knife River’s assets are economically underutilized. The business earns returns on invested capital (ROIC) of roughly 6–7%, less than its cost of capital. Without a meaningful margin of safety, incremental growth is unlikely to generate value for shareholders in the long term.

Market Expectations Analysis

A DCF model is not always reliable, but it can be reverse-engineered to get a rough view of the market’s expectations and see what’s baked into the current price of ~$72 per share (November 5th, 2025).

To avoid over-reliance on terminal values, I used a 30-year DCF model, finishing in 2055. Assuming an 80% cost of revenue margin, a corporate tax rate of 26%, reinvestment capex of 7%, a cost of capital of 9%, plus other assumptions for the income statement and the calculations of unlevered free cash flows, the market expects a cumulative annual growth rate of 3.4% over the next 30 years, which is in the range of 2% to 4% long-term growth rates for U.S. construction materials companies.

Knife River competes primarily with vertically integrated aggregates producers such as Vulcan Materials and Martin Marietta, which benefit from regional scale, pricing power, and superior margins, and can achieve north of 3.5% revenue CAGR over the long run. Martin Marietta, which has operating margins of ~17%, and Vulcan Materials, which has operating margins of ~15%, can produce CAGRs north of 3.5%. Knife River, as a recent standalone company with a smaller footprint and less diversified operations, has a limited ability to achieve similar cost efficiencies or pricing leverage compared to bigger competitors. In fact, Knife River would need a sustainable operating margin of ~16.5% to make its EPV equal to the reproduction cost of its assets NAV. That’s almost double the current sustainable operating margins of 9.5%, and since Knife River must compete with these larger rivals, which have superior scale and higher pricing power, doubling margins seems even more difficult.

The market is too optimistic about Knife River's future. It assumes high growth through acquisitions will drive incremental EBITDA, margin improvements from operational efficiencies, scale in aggregates, and increased pricing power. Revenues are increasing thanks to inorganic sales from the new acquisitions, but operating costs (costs of revenues and SG&A, which also include costs for M&A activities) are rising at a higher rate, creating margin constraints and reducing profitability. In my view, analysts focus too much on reported growth and EBITDA margins, which are short-term. They do not consider the industry's cyclicality, the capital needed for even moderate growth, and the limits on competitive returns.

(I didn't include the reverse DCF here as the data set is too big to be clear in a screen grab, but if anyone wants to take a look, I will gladly share it. Just let me know in the comments below.)

Risks

Even though the current fundamentals of the company warrant an underweight/sell rating, certain catalysts can make this stock rise further. These can be described in three positive scenarios. First is the fact that the gradual implementation and use of infrastructure funding from the federal government in 2026–2028 could help increase the sales volume of aggregate material and contracting services. The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA) may even sustain public construction demand longer than expected, which can benefit Knife River’s materials and contracting segments by boosting volume sales, revenue growth, and stability. Second, the successful integration of Strata Corporation, Albina Asphalt, and all the new acquisitions made in recent years could help expand EBITDA and operating margins, improving the profitability of the company, yielding stronger integration benefits and margin expansion than I previously thought. And lastly, if Knife River applies a stricter cost discipline policy, a reduction of capex in the following years, and better-than-expected weather patterns could help the company increase sustainable revenues and improve returns on invested capital. Also, if cost inflation eases, energy prices fall, and operating margins are reduced, Knife River could increase its earnings power and ROIC, narrowing the current gap between EPV and NAV.

Recommendation

I believe investors should be cautious when thinking about buying shares from Knife River. The bull case is that the company is going to grow revenues at comparable rates with competitors, thanks to their M&A and EDGE strategy. RBC Capital, which is giving one of the most optimistic price targets ($103 per share), is implying a CAGR of ~4.2%, while the market is expecting ~3.4% CAGR, which are both highly optimistic based on current fundamentals, little track record of Knife River, and the qualitative factors of this company (no competitive advantage).

The construction industry is already slowing down, proving the down cycle has begun, and the management is spending billions in new acquisitions that haven’t proved to provide profitable growth and are unlikely to provide value creation for shareholders in the long term, and even if Q3 2025 had record revenues, investors must not forget they also had record cost of revenues, which grew faster than revenues, and the management lowered guidance due to bad weather and lower demand, which reminds investors about the risks embedded in the industry.

At a fair value of ~$32 per share (on an equity basis, Knife River trades roughly ~122% above intrinsic value (as of November 7th, 2025)). Given weak capital efficiency, cyclical risk, and fading tailwinds, I rate the stock Underweight/Sell. New buyers should wait for a deep discount—around 2/3 of EPV (equity basis), or roughly $21 per share—before considering a position.

data.title: TON Strategy: Why I Bought Shares Despite Being A Crypto Skeptic
data.description:
Introduction

I am and have always been a crypto skeptic. I have never owned a cryptocurrency. My only exposure to the crypto sector has been via an investment in the largest US crypto exchange, Coinbase (NASDAQ:COIN), which has returned over 40% pa since 2022. However, this investment was based more on the intrinsic operating leverage within brokerage businesses and a bargain basement valuation in 2022 vs. deep rooted conviction in the growth potential of crypto as an asset class.

Despite my inherent skepticism of crypto, I recently initiated a starter position in TON Strategy Company (TONX). TON Strategy is a crypto treasury company for Toncoin, the native token of The Open Network (TON) - a layer-1 blockchain (like Ethereum or Solana) - that has an exclusive integration with the global messaging app Telegram.

Shares in TON Strategy have been on a wild ride over the past six months as the business has transitioned from a small legacy public company (Verb Technology) into a crypto treasury company. Shares are down 90% from their 52-week high in August 2025, presenting an opportunity for contrarian investors.

Why I Bought Shares

Below I detail why I was able to gain conviction to buy shares in TON Strategy, despite my inherent skepticism of the crypto industry.

1) Exclusive Telegram Partnership = Unmatched Distribution Advantages

The first thing that stood out to me about Toncoin was their exclusive partnership with Telegram. Below are some interesting statistics about Telegram, taken from a range of public interviews and podcasts with TON Strategy's Executive Chairman, Manuel Stotz:

  • 2nd largest global messaging platform and the 6th largest mobile app globally
  • Largest mobile app outside of the USA and China
  • Has over 1 billion monthly active users (expected to grow to 1.5 billion users over the next three years)
  • Has over 500 million daily active users
  • 80% of users are based in emerging or frontier markets

Telegram is an enormous company that rivals some of the largest US and Chinese tech companies for distribution and reach. If even 1% of Telegram's monthly active users used Toncoin for peer-to-peer payments, user numbers would already exceed those of most other cryptocurrencies.

Interestingly, Telegram is 100% owned by its founder (Pavel Durov) and has never raised external capital. Instead, Durov funded Telegram by selling down his longstanding personal stake in Bitcoin.

What makes Toncoin's partnership with Telegram most interesting is how it flips the growth equation for Toncoin. Most cryptocurrencies start with a product and then look for distribution, but Toncoin is the reverse - it starts with strong distribution via Telegram's base of 1 billion monthly active users. Over time, Toncoin will look to build a compelling suite of products to service the Telegram network.

2) Early, but Emerging Use Cases

One of my longstanding criticisms of cryptocurrencies has been the limited number of practical use cases (anyone remember monkey artwork NFTs?).

Right now, Toncoin is building out several use cases that have potential and make logical sense but are far from established. In short, the bet is that Toncoin will attract enough developer and investor attention to create genuine use cases within Telegram.

Current emerging use cases for Toncoin include:

  1. Supporting peer-to-peer payments via Telegram's in-built customer wallet (similar to a Venmo feature in the US)
  2. Sending gifts to other Telegram users
  3. Acting as the currency for Telegram's mini-app ecosystem (where developers can build apps natively within Telegram)
  4. Acting as the currency for businesses to advertise on Telegram (Telegram's core revenue stream in addition to paid subscriptions for premium messaging features)

In short, Toncoin's use cases are very early, but they have in-built distribution via their exclusive partnership with Telegram.

3) A High-Pedigree Executive Chairman

A big part of my thesis for TON Strategy is its Executive Chairman, Manuel Stotz. Stotz has a strong finance pedigree: he received a full scholarship to study at the London School of Economics, had internships at prestigious investment firms like Blackstone (NYSE:BX) and AKO Capital, and worked in the principal investments team at Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS). After leaving Goldman Sachs, Stotz built and scaled a multi-asset investment firm (Kingsway Capital) to US$5b AUM. Kingsway invests primarily in frontier markets like Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Nigeria, and offers a range of asset classes, including public equities, venture capital, and cryptocurrencies.

Stotz is a classic insider with blue-chip finance experience who has little incentive to go against the grain. He credited his interest in crypto (starting with Bitcoin but eventually leading to Toncoin) after seeing his investments at Kingsway Capital in frontier markets suffer from exposure to volatile and rapidly depreciating currencies.

While leading Kingsway Capital, Stotz became the biggest investor in Toncoin, which led him to become Chairman of the Ton Foundation and, in recent months, Executive Chairman of TON Strategy.

I've listened to all public interviews on Stotz and would highly recommend this interview on Blockchain and this interview on The Block. Stotz appears highly intelligent and a logical thinker who is aware of the limitations of crypto. He acknowledges that most cryptocurrencies will likely go to 0 but that Toncoin - due to their unique distribution advantages with Telegram - has the potential to become one of the few cryptocurrencies that matter.

4) Backed By Top-Tier Venture Capital Investors

In my day job, I work as a venture capitalist, so I was surprised to see in a March 2025 announcement that a number of top tier venture capital firms had directly invested into Toncoin (the cryptocurrency, not TON Strategy, the holdings company). These investors included:

  • Sequoia Capital
  • Benchmark
  • Ribbit Capital (specialist fintech investor)
  • Draper Associates

What is most interesting about this external investor validation is:

  1. All investors quoted in the article were high profile senior partners or founders of these investment firms
  2. Toncoin's price is down at least 30% since March 2025 (depending on when these investors bought Toncoin within the month)
  3. Most of these investors pulled back from crypto investments after the mania (and crash) of 2021/2022, so the fact that they backed Toncoin directly is a notable signal

5) An Attractive Permanent Capital Structure

TON Strategy is essentially a holdings company with the sole purpose of purchasing and holding Toncoin, benefiting from price appreciation in the underlying cryptocurrency and associated staking revenues.

In August 2025, TON Strategy raised a $558 million PIPE offering to build their Toncoin holdings. Most of this capital was used to acquire Toncoin with about $54 million kept on the balance sheet as cash or restricted cash.

TON Strategy's permanent capital structure is highly attractive and, if executed well, could lead to higher returns for public markets investors in TON Strategy than investors in Toncoin itself.

If shares trade at a premium to NAV (the total value of Toncoin on their balance sheet), TON Strategy can issue more shares at this premium and use the cash to purchase additional Toncoin at its market price. If shares trade at a discount to NAV (which is currently the case), TON Strategy can use excess cash and cash generated from staking to buy back shares at a discount to NAV. In Q3 2025, we saw TON Strategy repurchase over 2 million shares, which is over 3% of shares issued in their PIPE offering.

The company has a $250 million stock repurchase program lined up with Cantor Fitzgerald to execute as management sees fit.

6) Clean Balance Sheet

TON Strategy has a very clean balance sheet. The company has $54 million of cash (including restricted cash) and no debt. There is minimal goodwill and no material liabilities.

Stotz has stated publicly that TON Strategy will not use any 'hard leverage' (i.e., debt or convertible notes), which helps to de-risk the capital structure for equity investors.

7) Staking Revenues Provide Additional Upside

From my perspective, the core driver of future returns in TON Strategy will come from appreciation in the market price of Toncoin, so an investor would need to get conviction there is some fundamental reason why Toncoin should appreciate in price over the coming years. The exclusive partnership with Telegram and emerging use cases (including payments) are my anchor points, but other investors may disagree or have other takes.

TON Strategy also has another potential revenue stream from staking. In simplistic terms, TON Strategy can lock up their Toncoin to a validator on the Toncoin network who then verifies and secures the network and compensates TON Strategy in the form of more Toncoin. In a sense, this process works like a yield on a bond or deposit, albeit with more risk.

Current Toncoin staking rates are around the 3-4% mark, and TON Strategy has staked over 80% of their Toncoin balance. Using a 4% staking yield and TON Strategy's current staked balance, staking could generate at least $7 million of annualised revenue.

8) Material Discount to NAV

After coming to appreciate Stotz's vision for TON Strategy, the clincher for me to initiate a position was the large discount to NAV.

As of 19 November 2025, TON Strategy has a market cap of $182 million. With $53.9 million of cash and no debt, TON Strategy has an enterprise value of $128.1 million.

If we take TON Strategy's reported balance of Toncoin at 30 September 2025 of 217.8 million and the current Toncoin price of $1.79, we arrive at an estimated fair market value of $389.9 million of Toncoin.

This means that TON Strategy's enterprise value is 33% of the fair market value (i.e., NAV) of their Toncoin. In other words, TON Strategy trades at a 67% discount to the value of their Toncoin on the balance sheet.

Of course, some discount is warranted given that TON Strategy would not be able to liquidate their entire Toncoin balance (which represents just under 5% of all tokens in circulation) without materially affecting the price. However, this 67% discount is very high and reflects the market's skepticism for crypto-related businesses. Any narrowing of this discount provides substantial upside for equity investors.

9) Increasing Investor Access to Toncoin

While I place little weight in an investment thesis on investor psychology or fund flows, it is notable that overnight Toncoin became available for trading on Coinbase, the largest crypto exchange in the US. This means that Toncoin can be traded across major US crypto exchanges, including Coinbase, Gemini (NASDAQ:GEMI), Kraken, and Robinhood (NASDAQ:HOOD), which may help to bolster investment demand.

Risks

TON Strategy is the most speculative and highest risk public company I have written about. As such, TON Strategy accounts for <1% of my portfolio and is only a small starter position.

Core risks for the business include:

  1. General macro risks around crypto markets
  2. Toncoin's price is very volatile and can make quarter-to-quarter NAV results very noisy
  3. TON Strategy is a nascent company and only launched the treasury strategy in Q3 2025, so there is not a demonstrated track record of intelligent capital allocation
  4. Toncoin's use cases are very early and far from proven, with no guarantee of future customer adoption on the Telegram network.
  5. Telegram is a highly successful but controversial company that has attracted notable scrutiny from governments due to Durov's unwillingness to engage in political censorship, which may limit their user adoption in some markets

Conclusion

TON Strategy is a very unique company and the only publicly listed treasury holdings company for Toncoin. While I remain a strong crypto skeptic, it is hard to ignore the following:

  1. A very strong Executive Chairman (Manuel Stotz) who previously built a successful global investment firm
  2. Clear distribution advantages via their exclusive partnership with Telegram
  3. Emerging use cases
  4. The backing of top tier venture capital investors
  5. A clever permanent capital model that allows for value accretion via intelligent capital allocation (e.g., issuing shares at a premium to NAV, buying back shares at a discount to NAV)
  6. Clean balance sheet with no debt
  7. Trades at a 67% discount to the implied fair market value of their Toncoin holdings

Despite these positives, there are material risks with an investment in TON Strategy, and an investor's portfolio allocation should be small to reflect these risks.

data.title: Wayfair: Why You Should Not Buy The Stock In The Current Market
data.description:
Wayfair (W) is a well-known retailer in the online space offering furniture, décor, housewares, and home improvement products. The firm performed exceptionally well year-to-date, despite being in the consumer discretionary sector, which is often vulnerable during uncertain macroeconomic times. I believe that this outperformance is not likely to last, as the valuation stretched too far, as the stock price more than doubled this year.

The aim of this article today is to give you a balanced view on the pros and cons of investing in W's stock right now. I will be focusing on the macroeconomic environment, the latest quarterly results, as well as the valuation metrics.

Pros

1) Revenue Growth

One of the key pros for investing in W's stock is its compelling growth story. Based on the latest quarterly results, we can see that W achieved a topline growth of more than 8%. I believe this is a significant figure considering that W is selling discretionary products, which may not be so much in demand when the consumer sentiment is poor. (More on this later.) This growth would be even higher - 9% - if we excluded the Germany exit.

I also need to note that this revenue growth did not come from the expansion of accounts receivable. Sometimes, firms artificially inflate demand by selling more on credit, pulling revenue forward from future periods. This behavior can be detected by accounts receivable growing at a faster pace than revenue. This is not the case for W, however.

Also important to highlight that W's business grew not just in the U.S., but also internationally, despite the growth being only 4.6%, compared to the 8.6% growth in the United States.

Looking forward, I would like to see more aggressive international growth to give W a more geographically diversified customer base, though the recent exit from Germany does not point in this direction.

Other metrics that show the robust demand for W's products are:

  • 6.1% increase in LTM net revenue per active customer to $578.
  • 5.4% increase in orders delivered
  • New orders growing mid-single digits for two consecutive quarters

2) Improving margins

As a result of lower operating costs, especially lower advertising and lower SG&A expenses, W managed to generate a positive operating profit - $38 million in the prior quarter. In the past year, W's operating loss was $74 million.

Cons

1) Macroeconomic environment

Consumer sentiment in the United States is the worst it has been in years, and as a significant portion of W's revenue is coming from the U.S., it could be very important going forward.

Consumer confidence is often regarded as a leading economic indicator, which could be used to gauge the changes in the spending behaviour of the consumer in the coming months/quarters. When sentiment is poor, people are more uncertain about their financial stability and less likely to spend on expensive, discretionary, durable items, just like the ones that W sells.

While demand remained robust and W achieved topline growth, I believe that in the current market environment it is not sustainable. The health of the housing market also causes concerns for retailers selling furniture and home improvement products.

Existing home sales, despite slightly picking up in the previous months, remain low.

Although interest rates came down due to the recent Fed rate cuts, the extent and timing of rate cuts remain uncertain after the relatively hawkish remarks of Fed Chair Powell. With interest rate uncertainty ahead, I do not see a clear catalyst in the housing market and therefore for companies selling furniture, houseware, and home improvement products.

Weakness on the customer side is also visible in some metrics shared by W, e.g., the number of active customers declined by 2.3% YoY.

2) Net loss on a GAAP basis

Despite the topline growth and the improved profitability, W's business still generates a loss on the bottom line. Net loss was $99 million, compared to the net loss of $74 million in the prior year. However, I need to note that the net loss comes from the $99 million loss on debt extinguishment, which is likely a one-off item.

If we take the uncertainty in the macroeconomic environment into account and the high reliance on a U.S. customer base, I am not confident in saying that W's business will turn profitable in the near future.

3) Valuation

As W is still not profitable on a GAAP basis, the price multiples that can help us assess the company's valuation are P/S and P/CF. According to both metrics, W's stock appears to be overvalued. In terms of P/S, W's stock is roughly 18% more expensive than the respective sector median, while in terms of P/CF (TTM), W is selling at a 170% premium.

If I reduce the peer group to only firms in the home furnishing retail industry, W's stock still appears to be on the expensive side.

In my view, this premium is not justified in the current market environment.

I also need to mention that over the past decade, W was also diluting its shareholders by issuing shares. This is not uncommon for younger firms to raise capital, but normally I would like to see high-growth figures paired with it. The roughly 8% topline growth is not sufficient from my perspective.

4) Insider selling

Last, but not least, significant insider selling activity can be observed in the past weeks. I believe that when the CEO and Co-Founder, CTO, President, and CFO all sell at the same time, it cannot be an attractive time to buy.

Conclusion

W managed to deliver strong quarterly results, with 8% topline growth and a margin expansion, due to reduced operating costs. New orders also grew in the mid-single digits, and orders delivered increased by more than 5% YoY.

On the other hand, the current macroeconomic environment is creating significant headwinds for W's business, driven by the poor consumer sentiment and the weakness in the housing market. The valuation also seems to be stretched, as the P/S and P/CF ratios both indicate a premium compared to the peers.

Insider selling could also be a red flag for shareholders.

For these reasons, I rate W's stock as a sell now.

data.title: LyondellBasell Industries: Outlook Improving And 12% Yield Looks Sustainable
data.description:
Thesis

I earlier wrote about LyondellBasell Industries (LYB) in March this year. At that time the company was going through a difficult period due to industry headwinds. However, what intrigued me was that LYB offered an impressive dividend yield as well as valuations.

Since my last rating, the company continued its downtrend, with the company stock price dropping ~33% as compared to the S&P 500, which delivered a gain of ~16% for the same period. Naturally it has been quite disappointing for investors staying invested in the stock, but that's how investing works, especially for stocks going through industry headwinds.

On the flip side, the recent stock price decline has made LYB even more compelling as an investment. Plus, the company even yields a healthy 12% dividend yield now, which I believe is likely to be sustainable.

With respect to industry dynamics, management has turned optimistic in the last two quarters from not-so-encouraging comments in earlier quarters. While an increase in Chinese capacity can have a long-term headwind for LYB, I believe its lower cost structure insulates it from this headwind. Given all this, I am reiterating my buy rating on the stock.

Industry dynamics are improving, but we cannot call a bottom yet

Last time when I wrote about LYB in March, the company was going through a difficult period of demand headwinds, particularly due to oversupply in the industry and demand remaining muted. In fact, this has been the case since the second half of 2022. Prices for commodities such as ethylene and other downstream commodities produced by LYB have largely been in a downward trajectory and have witnessed destocking from the downstream industries

For most of the quarterly calls, including the first quarter of this year, management was not that upbeat about the industry outlook, with management referring to the outlook as uncertain and challenging. However, if we look at management’s tone regarding the industry outlook for the last two quarters, there has been a subtle shift from rather downbeat commentary to upbeat commentary.

The reason for the shift in tone is rather twofold: first, the demand has been rebounding for the last couple of quarters, and second, the industry capacities are now shutting down. The company noted positive (“strongest since the downtrend started in 2022,” as noted by management) in North America and Europe geographically in the polyethylene market. Year-to-date these geographies have grown from 2.5% to 3% from the same period in 2024.

Now, of course, a 2-3% growth rate is not that encouraging, but one should see it as a trend. Usually in commodities, volume and price ramp up slowly at the beginning, and when demand starts to outpace supply, players across the downstream industries rush to restock inventories. So yes, while these numbers might not look encouraging, the trend is what needs to be kept an eye on.

On the other end of the demand and supply balance, the petrochemical industry has been going through major capacity rationalization in the last few quarters. As per management, ~21 million tonne per annum worth of capacity for ethylene has been or is announced to be reduced from 2020 to 2028; that's almost 10% of the global demand for ethylene. Of these proposed capacity shutdowns, ~30% have been announced in the last 12 months, indicating an accelerated trend for capacity rationalization, which has been triggered by consistent industry pressure.

Now a counterargument to this is that while China has rationalized significant capacity in the last few years, they are also expanding their capacities. And they have been quite aggressive to ramp up these capacities. As per Reuters, China alone is expected to have ~21 MTPA worth of ethylene capacity by 2030. In other words, China is adding almost equal capacity to what is being rationalized. Given this trend, it makes more sense of the recent acceleration in rationalization.

LYB also is not unscathed from this aggressive Chinese expansion. LYB is also rationalizing its portfolio, particularly its high-cost operations in Europe. However, given LYB’s lower cost production in the US, I believe LYB is better placed to weather this industry headwind than other players.

Ethylene is produced either through cracking Natural Gas Liquids (NGLs) like Ethane or naphtha derived from Crude oil. LYB, or any US-based ethylene (feedstock for polyethylene) producer, cracks ethane to produce ethylene as opposed to European and Asian producers. This is primarily due to the abundant availability of ethane in the US thanks to the large domestic shale gas industries. Now the difference in production route creates a large difference in the cost structure of the ethylene producer. As per EIA, there is a significant cost advantage with US-based ethylene producers primarily due to the domestic ethane supply source.

So given the current downturn and expected Chinese capacity ramp-up, I am less worried about LYB’s future. Given LYB’s lower cost base in the US operations, I believe the company would weather the difficult times it is currently facing. While things could take time to turn around, usually in cyclical industries the winners are the ones who survive the challenging times, and US-based producers like LYB are apt for survival.

Capital allocation and dividend sustainability

Since LYB has a fair chance of not being as impacted by the current downtrend as other players in the industry, I believe LYB can consistently roll out dividends to its investors as it has historically done. While I would have preferred share repurchases now given its enticing valuations (more on the next segment), LYB, being a mature business with a wide dividend-seeking investor base, does prefer dividends over repurchases.

LYB currently has a dividend yield of ~12%; usually companies with such high dividend yields tend to be in a difficult situation and usually cut the dividends going forward. For LYB, I believe the case for a cut is less likely primarily due to LYB’s balance sheet strength and good cash flow even in challenging periods.

LYB’s debt metrics are healthy with a debt-to-equity ratio of ~1x with both debt and equity at ~$10 billion. If you add $1.7 billion of cash position, the ratio comes down further.

Furthermore, of this $11.8 billion debt ($10.7 billion long-term + $1.1 billion current portion of long-term debt), only ~$2 billion is expected to mature by 2029, which makes LYB’s liquidity position quite strong even if the industry downtrend extends long. Given its cash position, I believe LYB would largely use its current cash position to pay off the upcoming maturities till 2029.

Furthermore, the company is also planning to limit its capital outlay in coming years, down from ~$1.7 billion in 2025 to ~$1.2-1.4 billion in 2026.

Even in the current industry downtrend, LYB has managed to generate ~$2.7 billion in operating cash flow in TTM. Even if we assume TTM flow for 2026 the company would be largely able to pay off the projected capital outlay and ~$1.7 billion annual projected dividend payout. I know maintaining the current payout is a close call for management if the downtrend persists, but I believe the chance for a large dividend cut is unlikely at the current point in time.

LyondellBasell’s valuations are a lot more enticing now

I have talked about LYB’s dividend yield of 12% in the last segment, which in itself tells how inexpensive LYB’s current share price is. Similarly, LYB’s other valuation metrics, like price to tangible book value, are also at enticing levels.

For cyclical stocks like LYB I prefer to use the price-to-tangible book value (P/TBV) ratio primarily due to the fact that their revenue as well as profits tends to fluctuate dramatically over the course of a business cycle. For LYB its current P/TBV stands at ~1.5x, which is the lowest level seen in the last 10 years.

A 1x P/TBV ratio indicates a distressed valuation where a company’s market cap equals its assets minus liabilities. I believe LYB has a business that can generate supernormal profits primarily due to the fact that they can produce ethylene and downstream products at a much lower cost than the competitors in other parts of the world. This in itself should yield a premium to the company’s P/TBV multiple. However, the current 1.5x P/TBV is not factoring in this competitive advantage of LYB.

I believe this discount could persist during the industry downturn. However, if things improve from here, which seems to be happening in the recent quarters, LYB’s valuation ratio can rerate from here.

Risk

There are two primary risks to my thesis. First, the upcoming planned capacity expansion in China, which can erode margins in the market and extend the downtrend, and second, the stance of various governments around the world against the use of plastics.

Regions and countries around the world are becoming more aware and stringent with regard to the use of plastic or polyethylene. Even some countries are implementing bans on single-use plastic and encouraging the recycling of overused virgin resins. While this is definitely a headwind for the ethylene producers like LYB, this is not as substantial as it sounds. Yes, it could reduce the growth rate of the end market, but a substantial drop seems less likely.

Plastic is widely used in numerous end markets and usages, and replacing it in a cost-efficient manner is quite challenging. Even more so, industries such as the automobile industry are expected to increase the usage of plastic given the necessity to reduce weight for the EV transition.

Conclusion

Recent positive comments by management point toward improving industry dynamics. While Chinese capacity ramp-up can have a long-term headwind, I believe LYB’s lower cost structure insulates it from these headwinds. Given a lucrative 12% dividend yield, which I believe is likely sustainable, and cheap valuations, I am reiterating a buy rating for LYB.

data.title: Arista Networks: More Of A 2026 Opportunity Due To The Lagging Effect
data.description:
I last covered Arista Networks (ANET) in March 2024 in my piece entitled “AI Prospects Emerging But Will Take Time To Materialize,” mainly because of the time lag to recognize revenues between chip makers selling GPUs (accelerated compute) and those supplying switches.

Since then, the stock has more than doubled and was trading at $127 at the time of writing (chart below). However, it has been volatile despite recently delivering a beat during its third quarter 2025 results (Q3) but signaling modest sequential growth for Q4 and escalating deferred revenues.

Now, the company has doubled its AI ramp estimates for 2025 from $750 million to $1.5 billion, and OpenAI, having recently diversified its sourcing of compute silicon by striking deals with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Broadcom (AVGO), means additional sales opportunities for the networking play. Still, I have a Hold position, as despite the importance of networking in the AI infrastructure ecosystem, it faces a "lagging effect".

Networking is Key, but Lags AI Chips, Resulting in Deferred Revenues

Building large-scale AI infrastructure requires hundreds of thousands of chips, which must also be connected to data and, eventually, to users through the cloud. This type of connectivity is beyond traditional switches and requires high-speed networking and has to be scalable as AI models grow larger. In this respect, Arista is actively promoting open standards such as Ethernet, which is an alternative to proprietary technologies for building large AI clusters (or groups of interconnected GPUs), and Gartner has positioned it as a leader in data centers and cloud computing.

Next, looking at the AI infrastructure supply chain, companies like AMD and Broadcom come first as manufacturers of GPU chips and custom AI accelerators, respectively. Both can be used as alternatives for Nvidia's (NVDA) GPUs when it comes to AI inferencing, or actively using large language models after they have been trained. Here, the fact that OpenAI has partnered with these two shows that the semiconductor giant simply cannot satisfy the demand, which is good for the AI infrastructure in general.

Coming to Arista's opportunities, in addition to 10 GW equivalent of AI accelerator chips by 2029, OpenAI's partnership with Broadcom will also see the latter handling the networking systems. Digging deeper, it produces the silicon that Arista fits inside its switches, but only after testing and validation, which takes time. Furthermore, hyperscalers like Microsoft (MSFT), Oracle (ORCL), or Meta Platforms (META) first have to secure a supply of GPUs (as key ingredients for powerful language models) before ordering network devices from companies like Arista to interconnect them, which again shows that it may take longer for AI sales to materialize. Thus, the networking play's revenue growth lags chip plays.

Furthermore, this lagging effect is a driver for Arista's deferred sales, as I detail below, and may have contributed to the stock's recent volatility. However, looking beyond these variabilities, there are opportunities. In this connection, Nvidia's proprietary InfiniBand technologies, sold as part of a bundle when selling its GPUs, tend to dominate back-end networks (for connecting GPU-based servers to one another and to the data needed for building language models). However, this could change since OpenAI’s custom chips will use Broadcom interconnects, making Arista’s AI switches a logical fit, making the company an indirect beneficiary as vendors other than Nvidia emerge.

New Opportunities, but There is Competition

Now, Arista has been positioning itself for high-speed switching for AI clusters since the CEO, Jayshree Ullal, confirmed in the Q3-2024 earnings call back in November last year that they were building systems using Broadcom’s Tomahawk 5 and Jericho 3 chips for AI Ethernet fabrics.

Furthermore, looking at the agreement AMD signed with OpenAI for the supply of 6GW equivalent of accelerated compute involving its MI450 GPUs, these need to be interconnected by ultra-fast Ethernet fabrics. However, unlike Nvidia, AMD does not have high-speed networking capability in-house, implying an expanded TAM for Arista, especially those customers choosing open Ethernet over proprietary technology.

Thus, Arista should indirectly benefit from Nvidia no longer monopolizing the GPU world, as AI capex now increasingly includes networking. This means additional sales opportunities and explains why Wall Street is mostly bullish, with an average price target of nearly $164, representing a 25% upside for a company that enjoys exceptionally high margins.

However, as evidenced by the valuation grade above, the stock remains richly valued and could suffer from further downside when considering risks.

First, even if the AI backend network market is rapidly expanding and expected to grow by 50% CAGR from 2025 to 2028 to reach $20 billion, there is competition, notably from established players like Nvidia with its Spectrum X Ethernet-based systems, in addition to InfiniBand. At the same time, Cisco (CSCO) has positioned itself with Silicon One, and there are several white-box vendors. The latter are strong competitors in front-end networking markets (which mostly link CPU-based hosts to each other, to storage, and to the cloud for user access) primarily because they provide highly customizable solutions in contrast to Arista's more integrated products, which are not suitable for customers bent on avoiding vendor lock-in.

Second, again looking at the recent volatility, it has been caused by sales decelerating sequentially (from 10% QoQ in Q2 to 5% QoQ in Q3) and is estimated to further drop to 2% in Q4. This means a lower pace of revenue conversions (or deferred sales recognized in the income statement) due to delayed deployment at the client's premises. Also, the deferred sales of $3.5 billion at the end of Q3 urge caution since the management mentions problems of timing and variability.

The Stock Deserves Better Despite the Variability Risks, but This is for Next Year

This means the $3.35 EPS estimated for 2026 does not account for deferred revenues being converted to profits because, despite the switches having already been delivered and costs already recognized, dollars do not flow into Arista’s income statement since the deployment takes time, probably because customers first have to energize the GPU clusters, which takes time because of power constraints.

Still, the management insists that there is no deceleration in demand. Also, one should not ignore the opportunities after Broadcom and AMD GPU ecosystems potentially tilting Arista's product mix toward high-margin back-end Ethernet fabrics. This justifies a bullish EPS revision for FY26.

Now, Arista’s short-term deferred revenue of $3.52B, being 33% of FY26 revenue guidance, appears exceptionally high. If FY26 recognizes even 16% (about half of 33%) more of deferred revenue than the cautious guidance allows, additional sales of around $563 million (3,520 x 0.16) become feasible. This in turn leads to an incremental net income of around $225 million (563 x 0.4) based on margins of roughly 40% for FY24. Next, dividing this figure by its diluted weighted average shares outstanding for the period ending December 2024 of 1.28 billion yields an additional EPS of around $0.18 (225/1280) being added to FY26. This results in a new EPS of $3.53 (3.35 + 0.18) and a share price estimate of $138 (3.53 x 39.17) based on a forward P/E of 39.17x.

This represents an upside of only around 8.5% ((138-127)/127) over the next 12 months from the current share price, explaining my neutrality. To justify my position, there are downside risks because of increasing whispers about an AI bubble, and these should persist even after Nvidia announced upbeat fiscal third-quarter results on Wednesday, as investors' attention should increasingly focus on ROI, given the hundreds of billions of dollars of capital expenses being dedicated to top-tier infrastructure. Specifically for Arista, this may mean even more volatility because of the lagging effect, which can cause hyperscalers to postpone cluster commissioning, delaying revenues.

Deferred Revenues Provide Visibility, but Volatility Should Persist

However, customers cannot continuously delay cluster deployments, and Arista possesses several strengths.

First, despite being detrimental to earnings, deferred revenue provides visibility, and the company forecasts its AI networking revenue to grow significantly, from $1.5 billion in 2025 (versus the initial estimate of $750 million) to $2.75 billion in 2026, showing that it is successfully capturing value in this high-growth market.

Second, with strong net income margins as per the table below (after excluding Nvidia, which is currently benefiting from a near-monopolistic position) and solid cash conversion with an FCF margin that exceeds the IT sector by more than 230%, it implies Arista has the financial clout to take on white-box players like Celestica (CLS), incumbents like Cisco (CSCO), or Nvidia through its data center product called Spectrum-X based on Ethernet technology.

Third, given its strong track record in software-defined networking, Arista has positioned itself as the "go-to supplier" for data center switches for hyperscalers, including Microsoft and Meta Platforms. Thus, it is a natural choice with AI-defined networking.

Fourth, Arista differentiates itself with its Extensible Operating System (EOS), which is provided for free with basic functions, but whose advanced paid features, like automation and troubleshooting tools, can prove especially useful for client datacenters lacking the resources required to build their own network systems.

Finally, sticking to the lagging effect narrative, I have made an investment case, but this is based on deferred sales diminishing in 2026 and conditional on Arista achieving the roughly $1 billion revenue recognition next year. Also, while it should benefit from some upward momentum in the aftermath of Nvidia's record-breaking financial results, the stock will continue to be volatile, as mentioned earlier, meaning that some may prefer to wait on the sidelines for a better margin of safety. This said, there are long-term opportunities as the company expects to see uptake for its Scale-Up Ethernet (developed in partnership with Broadcom) in 2027 for high-speed data center connectivity purposes.
